role_summary: President of the Forrest County, Mississippi chapter of the NAACP; killed January 10, 1966 in a Ku Klux Klan firebombing of his Hattiesburg home, the violence his wife Ellie Dahmer (catalog entry 36) and son Vernon Dahmer Jr. (catalog entry 121) survived.
ai_reading: Dahmer Sr. is the subject of both the Ellie Dahmer (entry 36) and Vernon Dahmer Jr. (entry 121) catalog pages, where the January 10, 1966 firebombing is the anchor event. He is not himself a Civil Rights History Project interviewee (his murder predated the project by four decades); his standalone catalog page exists so his own name is findable alongside his survivors' pages and so the FBI Bowers-prosecution arc has a third anchor in the catalog. The eventual 1998 state-court conviction of Imperial Wizard Samuel Bowers for ordering the firebombing was the close of one of the longest-running cold-case prosecutions of the civil rights era; the Dahmer family interviewees (Ellie and Vernon Jr) speak to that thirty-two-year wait.
biographical_paragraph: Dahmer was born March 10, 1908 in Forrest County, Mississippi and built a successful livelihood as a farmer, sawmill operator, grocer, and cotton ginner outside Hattiesburg. He served as president of the Forrest County NAACP and, beginning in 1962, allowed African American voters to pay their poll taxes at his grocery store, a service his wife Ellie noted he announced publicly in the days before his death. On the night of January 10, 1966 Ku Klux Klansmen firebombed his home; Dahmer returned fire from inside as his wife and children escaped through a window, but he was overcome by burns and smoke inhalation and died the following day at the Hattiesburg hospital. The killers were members of the White Knights of the Ku Klux Klan, the Mississippi faction whose Imperial Wizard Samuel Bowers had also ordered the 1964 Mississippi Burning murders of Chaney, Goodman, and Schwerner. Federal civil rights charges produced four convictions in 1968; state murder charges against Bowers were retried four times before a 1998 conviction. Dahmer is buried in the family cemetery in the Kelly Settlement community in Forrest County.

sources.title: Vernon Dahmer murder case archival record (FBI Mississippi Burning-era civil rights case files)
sources.publisher: Federal Bureau of Investigation, Famous Cases archives
